questions.text: I believe that if I don't invest in myself, no one else will.
I would move to a less desirable place if it let me be more useful to people who need help.
I prefer to build agreement organically rather than persuade.
I regularly push back when I disagree with the prevailing direction.
I pick up on when a conversation is being steered toward a predetermined conclusion.
I pick up on when a shared laugh is a bonding moment versus a nervous release.
I value precision in my work and take time to get the details right.
I believe my vivid emotional life is what makes me feel most alive.
I often find myself telling others what needs to happen next.
I hold the view that self-sacrifice is one of the highest forms of contribution.
When something delights me, the delight fills me completely.
I believe that the moral demand of suffering is most authentic when it leads to a change in what I do, not just what I feel.
I proactively organize shared spaces and group projects because order enables progress.
I can receive bad news without my body going into alarm mode.
I feel that moral seriousness about suffering means being willing to stay engaged with it long after the emotional intensity has faded.
I find that skipping the planning phase allows me to be more present during execution.
I find that I make better decisions about how to help when I can maintain emotional distance from the pain I am witnessing.
I tend to treat all conversations as primarily about exchanging information.
